footer_tagline: The most gaúcho city in Rio Grande do Sul — tradition, frontier and innovation at the heart of the Pampa.
footer_madeby: Made with pride in the Pampa
footer_disclaimer: Information gathered from public and official sources — please confirm before visiting.
home_hero_lead: The “most gaúcho” city in Rio Grande do Sul and the largest municipality by land area in the state. Frontier tradition, a strong agribusiness and a growing innovation ecosystem — all on one mighty stretch of land.
home_stat_terr: Largest municipality in RS by area
home_about_p1: On the banks of the Ibirapuitã River, Alegrete keeps the soul of Rio Grande do Sul alive: the barbecue, the mate, the horse, the poetry and the hospitality of frontier life. It was the Third Capital of the Riograndense Republic and the birthplace of poet Mário Quintana and statesman Oswaldo Aranha.
home_about_p2: Today the city blends the strength of the countryside with technology — innovation parks, events that span generations, and a landscape that invites you to slow down. This portal brings it all together — for visitors, for locals, and for those thinking of staying.
home_explore_p: Ten ways into the city and its region — pick yours.
exp_history_d: Farrapos, missions and the 3rd Capital of the Republic.
exp_food_d: Barbecue, campfire sausage and the best tables in town.
exp_stay_d: Hotels, inns and camping for every style.
exp_sports_d: Running, padel, kayaking, courts and horse riding.
exp_nature_d: Rivers, Pampa reserves and ecological trails.
exp_events_d: Sausage Festival, Campereada and Farroupilha Week.
exp_shopping_d: Free shops in Rivera, Uruguaiana and Artigas.
exp_region_d: Uruguay, Argentina and the Campanha wineries.
home_events_p: Tradition, flavor and sport fill Alegrete's calendar.
home_links_p: Official sources and local profiles with up-to-date, day-to-day information.
hist_lead: From the Jesuit missions to the Farroupilha Revolution: the story of a people who rebuilt their city more than once.
hist_p1: Alegrete's origins date back to 1626, tied to the Spanish Jesuit missions. The land belonged to the Yapeyú reduction before being claimed for the Portuguese crown in 1801.
hist_p2: The city's history is one of resilience: after the first settlement (1812) was burned by followers of José Artigas — the “Burned Chapel” episode — the people rebuilt today's town on the banks of the Ibirapuitã River.
hist_capital_p: During the Farroupilha Revolution (1835–1845), Alegrete became the Third Capital of the Riograndense Republic and hosted Brazil's first Republican Constituent Assembly, in 1842.
hist_people_p: Alegrete is the hometown of poet Mário Quintana (1906), one of the great names of Brazilian literature, and of diplomat and statesman Oswaldo Aranha. It is also known as the cradle of the Canto Alegretense and of leading figures of gaúcho traditionalism.
hist_mon_p: Discover the historic Borges de Medeiros Bridge (scene of the 1923 battle), the Gothic-style Mother Church (1919), the century-old Railway Station with its steam engine, and the Negrinho Triunfante monument by sculptor Vasco Prado.
hist_museum_p: The Oswaldo Aranha Museum holds the famous diplomat's collection. Regional culture also lives on at the Gaúcho Museum and the city's cultural centers, alongside the 16 CTGs that keep tradition alive.
hist_mil_p: Alegrete hosts one of the country's largest military contingents, with visits to combat equipment, an equestrian season and parades that reinforce the city's frontier vocation.
food_lead: The capital of campfire sausage: open-fire barbecue, charque, artisan cheeses and the best of Pampa cuisine.
food_typ_p: Alegrete's cuisine is pure Pampa: meat, fire, abundance and mate always within reach.
food_t1_p: The star of the gaúcho table: prime cuts slow-roasted over embers, with coarse salt and patience.
food_t2_p: Alegrete is the State Capital of Campfire Sausage, with artisan production of hundreds of tons a year.
food_t3_p: Estancia heritage: charque (jerked beef), arroz de carreteiro and the feijão-tropeiro that fed generations of ranch hands.
food_t4_p: Dulce de leche, ambrosia and the inevitable round of chimarrão to end — or start — the day.
food_places_p: A selection of addresses with contact and location. Tap a card to call, follow on Instagram or get directions.
stay_lead: From downtown comfort to country inns: where to rest after a day in the Pampa.
stay_p: Options for every budget and style — from business hotels to rural tourism and motorhomes.
sports_lead: Runs across the Pampa, padel, kayaking on the Ibirapuitã, horse riding and courts for the usual pickup game.
sports_p: An active city all year round: from asphalt to water, from field to court.
sports_run_p: The Independence Half Marathon and circuits like the Conesul draw runners from across the region.
sports_padel_p: Padel is booming, adding to the football (society), volleyball and tennis courts spread across the city.
sports_water_p: The Ibirapuitã River is a stage for kayaking, stand up paddle and rowing — nature and adrenaline minutes from downtown.
sports_horse_p: Equestrian seasons, lasso contests and bronc riding keep the alegretense bond with the horse alive.
nature_lead: The Pampa at its purest: rivers to fish and paddle, the biome's only federal reserve and trails crossing the Campanha.
nature_p: Grasslands, wetlands, gallery forests and vast skies — the Pampa invites you to slow down.
nature_river_p: The river that embraces the city is perfect for fishing, kayaking, stand up paddle and sunset boat rides.
nature_apa_p: At around 317,000 hectares, it is the only federal Environmental Protection Area in the Pampa biome, sheltering grasslands, wetlands and native wildlife.
nature_trail_p: The Caminho do Pampa, a long-distance trail, runs through the EPA with dozens of kilometers already mapped for sustainable tourism.
nature_birds_p: The grasslands and wetlands are a paradise for birdwatchers, rich in Pampa bird species.
events_lead: Tradition, flavor and sport all year long — the calendar that makes Alegrete pulse.
events_p: The dates that bring locals and visitors together around gaúcho culture.
events_note: Dates may vary each edition — confirm on official channels.
shop_lead: On the Mercosur triple frontier: free shops, perfumes, wines and electronics just kilometers from Alegrete.
shop_p: Alegrete is the perfect base for a day trip to the twin cities of Uruguay and Argentina.
shop_tip1_p: Bring a valid ID card or passport. Minors require specific documentation.
shop_tip2_p: The land allowance is US$500 per person. Above that, you must declare and pay tax on return.
shop_tip3_p: Free shops usually accept reais, US dollars and cards. Compare prices and the day's exchange rate.
region_lead: From the Campanha wine route to Uruguay's hot springs and Argentina's historic cities — all within reach of Alegrete.
region_p: Day trips or weekend routes starting from the Baita Chão.
region_wine_p: The region is Brazilian wine's new frontier. Visit Almadén in Santana do Livramento (the country's largest vineyard) and Guatambu in Dom Pedrito — with tours, tastings and the Pampa Train.
region_uy_p: Rivera and its shopping, the hot springs of Salto and, farther on, Montevideo — capital of beaches and River Plate history.
region_ar_p: Paso de los Libres, neighboring Corrientes and vibrant Buenos Aires, all connected through the Uruguaiana border.
getting_lead: How to get in and out: highways, buses, airports and the distances that connect Alegrete to the world.
getting_air_p: Gaudêncio Machado Ramos Airport sits by km 592 of the BR-290, about 12 km from downtown. For regular commercial flights, the airports of Uruguaiana, Santa Maria and Porto Alegre (Salgado Filho) are the main gateways.
getting_road_p: Alegrete sits on the Mercosur corridor: the BR-290 links the city to Porto Alegre and the Uruguaiana border, complemented by the RS-377 and RS-566.
getting_bus_p: The Bus Station connects Alegrete to Porto Alegre (about 7h) and Uruguaiana (about 2h, several daily departures). From Uruguaiana — a hub equidistant from Porto Alegre, Montevideo and Buenos Aires — international lines depart to Argentina and Uruguay.
